title: Laws of AI Agents
subtitle: Hard-won heuristics for building agents that actually work.
intro: Not proven theorems — field notes. Every law here comes from something that bit me while shipping agents. Inspired by the format of Laws of UX, but the scars are my own.

categories.name: Context & Reliability
categories.blurb: What makes agents work or fail.

laws.name: Law of Context Decay
laws.tagline: Agents fail at context, not reasoning.
laws.principle: Most bad outputs trace to missing, stale, or poisoned context — not a dumb model. The model is usually smart enough; it was just working from the wrong picture of the world.
laws.takeaway: Before blaming the model, audit what it could actually see. Fix the context window before you reach for a bigger model.

laws.name: The Tool Description Is the Prompt
laws.tagline: An agent is only as capable as its tools are legible.
laws.principle: Vague tool descriptions cause more failures than vague instructions. The agent decides what to call based on how the tool reads, not on what it actually does.
laws.takeaway: Write tool descriptions like you are onboarding a new engineer: what it does, when to use it, what it returns, what it costs.

laws.name: Compounding Error Law
laws.principle: A 95% step run ten times is a coin flip. Every added step lowers the ceiling, and the failures don't announce themselves — they quietly accumulate until the final answer is wrong.
laws.takeaway: Count your steps. Shorten the chain, raise per-step reliability, or checkpoint so a bad step can't poison the rest.

laws.name: Narrow Beats General
laws.tagline: A scoped agent with three tools beats a generalist with thirty.
laws.principle: Capability surface is liability surface. Every extra tool is another way to pick wrong, another branch to test, another failure to debug.
laws.takeaway: Start narrow. Add a tool only when a real task demands it, not because it might be useful someday.

laws.name: Determinism at the Edges
laws.tagline: Model in the middle, code at the boundaries.
laws.principle: Validation, schemas, retries, and routing are not the LLM's job. The model is for judgment; deterministic code is for everything that must be correct every time.
laws.takeaway: Wrap the model in code you can trust. Let it reason, but never let it be the thing that guarantees a contract.

laws.name: The Model Optimizes for Looking Done
laws.principle: They'll write the summary before doing the work unless forced to prove it. "Looking finished" is cheaper than being finished, and the model will take the cheaper path.
laws.takeaway: Demand evidence, not assertions. Make the agent show the artifact — the test pass, the file, the diff — before it claims success.

laws.name: Observability Precedes Autonomy
laws.tagline: You can't grant autonomy you can't trace.
laws.principle: No traces, no trust. If you can't see what the agent did and why, you can't safely let it act on its own — you're just hoping.
laws.takeaway: Build the trace before the autonomy. Every decision, tool call, and input should be inspectable after the fact.

laws.name: The Handoff Is the Hard Part
laws.tagline: In multi-agent systems, failures live in the seams.
laws.principle: What gets passed, what gets dropped, who owns state — the bug is rarely inside an agent, it's between them. Each agent looks fine in isolation and the system still breaks.
laws.takeaway: Design the contract at every handoff. Make state ownership explicit and validate what crosses the boundary.

laws.name: Trust Is Calibrated, Not Granted
laws.tagline: Users extend autonomy in proportion to track record.
laws.principle: Trust is earned on reversible actions before irreversible ones. People don't hand an agent the keys on day one — they widen the leash as it proves itself.
laws.takeaway: Earn autonomy incrementally. Start with reversible, low-stakes actions and expand the blast radius only as reliability is demonstrated.

laws.name: The Escape Hatch Law
laws.tagline: No clean path to a human means a hallucinated one.
laws.principle: An agent with no clean "hand to a human" path will invent a path instead. Cornered without an exit, it fabricates an answer rather than admitting it's stuck.
laws.takeaway: Always give the agent a way out. A first-class "I need a human" action beats a confident wrong answer every time.
